diagrams.title: Binary Heap Array Representation
diagrams.description: Shows the correspondence between a binary tree max-heap and its array representation. The tree structure demonstrates parent-child relationships (parent at i, children at 2i+1 and 2i+2), while the array shows index positions. This dual representation is essential for implementing heapsort efficiently in-place.
elements: Binary tree with max-heap property (parent ≥ children)
Array representation with index labels (0 to 9)
Arrows connecting tree nodes to array positions
diagrams.purpose: Explain the fundamental data structure used in heapsort - how a complete binary tree maps to an array for O(1) parent/child access

diagrams.title: Max-Heapify Operation (Sift-Down)
diagrams.description: Demonstrates the heapify process on a subtree where the max-heap property is violated. Shows how comparing a node with its children and swapping with the larger child restores the heap property recursively. This is the core operation used during both heap construction and sorting.
elements: Initial subtree with root smaller than one or both children
Comparison indicators (arrows showing what gets compared)
Recursive call to heapify on affected subtree
diagrams.purpose: Illustrate the key operation that maintains the max-heap property, explaining how O(log n) work per sift-down enables the overall O(n log n) complexity

diagrams.title: Heapsort Algorithm Steps
diagrams.description: Complete visual walkthrough of heapsort: (1) Build max-heap from unsorted array, (2) Swap root (max) with last element, (3) Reduce heap size and heapify root, (4) Repeat until sorted. Shows both tree and array views progressing through each step.
elements: Swap animation between root and last unsorted element
Sorted portion growing from right to left
diagrams.purpose: Visualize the complete heapsort algorithm flow, demonstrating how elements are extracted in descending order and placed in their final sorted positions, making the O(n log n) time complexity intuitive
